summary: I grew up in Ogdensburg, New York, right up on the St. Lawrence River. It was a small town where I finished high school in 1947 before heading off to forestry school. My brother also came from Ogdensburg to visit me during Christmas in 1950 when I was preparing for overseas deployment.

Ogdensburg, where I grew up, is located on the St. Lawrence River. I remember the river as part of my hometown environment in northern New York. It was a significant geographical feature of the area where I spent my early years.

After high school, I attended a two-year forestry school in the Adirondack Mountains, graduating in May 1949. It was a beautiful and rugged area, perfect for forestry studies, but I wasn’t sure if I wanted to pursue forestry as a career. That uncertainty led me to enlist in the army shortly after.

I enlisted in the army in Syracuse, New York on July 1st, 1949. It was a peaceful time with no hint of the Korean conflict ahead. Syracuse was the place where I made the decision to join the military after finishing my forestry degree.

When the Korean War started in 1950, I was at Fort Lewis, Washington. At first, none of us had any idea how big or serious the conflict would become. I remember the North Koreans pushing down toward Kaesong and the activation of National Guard units, but the full scale of the war was still unclear to us.

I went through basic training at Fort Dix, New Jersey, from July to September 1949. The first sergeant was tough but memorable, and it was at Fort Dix that I saw my first television. I also had a chance to visit relatives on Staten Island during training, where a kind lady paid my ferry fare, a moment I never forgot.

During basic training, I got to spend a weekend in New York City, which was a big deal for me since I had only been there as a child. I remembered the peaceful Sunday mornings and was awestruck by the Empire State Building’s height. The city left a lasting impression on me, especially the quiet mornings and the grandeur of the buildings.

As a child, I visited the New York World's Fair in 1940. That was one of my early memories of New York City before I joined the army. It was a significant event that I recalled when I later returned to the city during my training.

Seeing the Empire State Building for the first time during my weekend in New York City was awe-inspiring. It was so tall that I got a crick in my neck just looking up at it. That image stuck with me as a symbol of the city’s grandeur.

After enjoying New York City on the weekend, I took the train back from Grand Central Terminal to Princeton, New Jersey, and then returned to Fort Dix. It was part of the routine during my basic training period and a memorable transit point in the city.

During my army training, I traveled through Princeton, New Jersey, when returning from New York City to Fort Dix. It was a stopover point on the train route and part of my early army experience in 1949.

After basic training, I was assigned to Fort Lewis, Washington, where I trained in field artillery starting in late 1949. Fort Lewis was also where I was when the Korean War broke out in 1950, and where I helped register National Guard and reserve soldiers being activated. It was a pivotal place in my military career.

On the train ride across the country to Fort Lewis, we stopped in Chicago for about half a day. I took the opportunity to walk along the lakefront and visit some museums. It was a memorable break on a long journey in 1949.

General Douglas MacArthur was the Supreme Commander in the Pacific during the post-World War II occupation of Japan when I was in the army. His leadership was well known, and it was under his command that the U.S. maintained control in Japan before the Korean War escalated. His presence was a backdrop to the early days of my service.

I enlisted in the United States Army in 1949 and served through the Korean War period. My roles included basic training at Fort Dix, artillery training at Fort Lewis, and later deployment overseas. The army shaped much of my early adult life and gave me experiences I still recall vividly.

During the Korean War, many National Guard units were activated and processed through Fort Lewis where I was stationed. I helped register these soldiers, witnessing the emotional farewells of older men and their families. It was a sobering experience that showed the human side of military mobilization.

Kaesong was a key location during the early Korean War, where the North Koreans formed a perimeter as they advanced south. I remember hearing about the Kaesong perimeter while I was still training at Fort Lewis in 1950. It marked one of the first major fronts in the conflict.

I was sent to Fort Sill, Oklahoma, for survey school as part of my artillery training. It was there that my brother came to visit me for Christmas in 1950, just before I was ordered to report for overseas shipment. Fort Sill was an important step before heading into active service abroad.

I was assigned to the 204th Field Artillery Battalion, a unit mainly composed of Mormons from Logan, Utah. They were some of the finest men I ever met, and the commander, Colonel Joseph, was a great leader. We shipped out together in January 1951 for Korea.

Logan, Utah, was the home of most of the men in the 204th Field Artillery Battalion. The unit was largely made up of Mormons from that area, and I found their camaraderie and discipline impressive. It was a unique cultural aspect of my military experience.

The 204th Field Artillery Battalion was predominantly Mormon, which influenced the unit’s character and cohesion. Although it wasn’t by design, most soldiers were from Mormon backgrounds around Logan, Utah. I never encountered better men than those in that unit.

We sailed to Japan aboard the USS General Anderson in January 1951. The ship had different classes of passengers, with us common soldiers in the lowest class. I remember the rough seas, the cramped bunks, and the humorous moments like watching pancake batter spill on deck during a roll.

Our ship arrived in Japan at Sansapaul, which was quiet except for the sudden arrival of high-ranking officers for a summit. Japan was a staging area before heading into Korea, and it was under U.S. occupation at the time. It was my first exposure to a foreign culture during the war.

Sansapaul was the Japanese port where our ship docked on the way to Korea. It was quiet until officers arrived for a meeting, and we witnessed limousines being unloaded. It was a brief but memorable stop on our journey in early 1951.

I was deployed to the Republic of Korea during the Korean War as part of the 204th Field Artillery Battalion. Arriving in early 1951, I faced the realities of a foreign country at war. It was a challenging adjustment but a defining part of my military service.

Chosen was mentioned as part of the Korean region during the war, though I did not have direct experience there. It was part of the broader theater of operations in Korea at the time. My focus was more on areas like Kaesong and Seoul during my deployment.

While I was training and preparing for deployment, the Battle of Inchon was a major turning point in the Korean War. I didn’t participate directly in the battle, but it was a significant event that shaped the course of the conflict we were entering. It was part of the news and military discussions at the time.

Seoul was a key city in the Korean War and part of the area where fighting took place. Although I did not describe being there specifically, it was central to the conflict and mentioned in the context of the war’s progress. It was one of the major objectives during the campaign.
